paras.t: Welcome — find a seat, grab some snacks.   WiFi:   ·   Password:
Tech:NYC’s Decoded Futures:  the programme we’re modelled on, 220+ nonprofits served in New York.
Prof. Rajiv Joshi, Columbia University and Bridging Ventures NYC.
9:30  —  What to expect across the cohort.
4:45  —  Show & tell — demo what you built today.
We’re going to spend a lot of time together over the next two months, let’s start with some introductions!!
Let’s take 5 minutes to talk to each other across the table.
Something that excites you, or worries you about AI.
Skepticism is welcome here. Naming a worry is as useful as naming a hope.
A century ago, electricity went from a curiosity to the quiet power behind every industry. AI is on the same path — a general-purpose capability you plug into almost any kind of work.
“AI is the new electricity.” — Andrew Ng
Where there’s hype, there’s a hustle
“Become an AI millionaire — only ₹9.” Hype grows its own economy of shortcuts and gurus. Real skill isn’t for sale at a discount — it’s built by doing, which is exactly why you’re here.
Where there’s hype, there’s a hustle
“Become an AI millionaire — only ₹9.” Hype grows its own economy of shortcuts and gurus. Real skill isn’t for sale at a discount — it’s built by doing, which is exactly why you’re here.
What  learns from past data to forecast and classify.
Asks  “what’s likely?” — fraud, no-shows, a diagnosis.
In the wild  predicting how proteins fold to design new medicines.
Strongest with good data and a clear question.
What  makes something new from a prompt — text, images, code.
Asks  “make me one” — a draft, a summary, a picture.
In the wild  Google’s “Nano Banana” turns a sentence into an image.
You steer; it produces the first draft.
You know your work better than anyone in this room. Our job isn’t to tell you what to build, it’s to help you put AI to the work you already do.
What teams build in a programme like this
Build a policy  —  a usable internal AI policy for your org.
Automate the admin  —  reclaim hours from repetitive tasks.
Create & communicate  —  draft materials and content faster.
Answer & assist  —  handle repetitive questions at scale.
Learn & decide  —  turn your own data into insight.
We’re a first cohort, so we’re learning together but the destination is real, working tools you own and run.
From friction in your day-to-day to a tool you can run.
Explore where AI can help and build hands-on fluency before committing to a problem.
Build fluency with prompting and core tools
Spot workflows that feel unclear or manual
Narrow from many ideas to one clear, AI-solvable workflow.
Narrow to one workflow, your MVP
Describe what success looks like, and how you’ll judge it
Turn the scoped workflow into a reusable AI prototype.
Build a reusable prototype aligned to the workflow
Compare outputs to your success criteria and refine
Make it consistent, reliable, and ready for real use.
Find edge cases and failure modes
Clarify how it fits your team’s decisions and workflow
Show what you built and how it changes the work.
Present the workflow, prototype, and evidence of impact
Outline next steps for adoption and iteration
Where it all leads : DEMO DAY!!
Four in-person sessions. One destination - 25th July 2026
A Celebration with your AI Solution Demos on how you’re enhancing impact and workflows
Built by your team, for your team.
Building fluency takes time  —  nobody walks in fluent; that’s the point of two months.
You learn best by doing  —  we build more than we lecture.
The fun comes in iterating  —  the second and third tries are where it gets good.
Guardrails first and then we experiment freely.
We’re navigating a new frontier together. None of us has all the answers yet — and that’s exactly why we start by agreeing on how we’ll move.
Adopt AI in the way that’s right for your organisation.
01  Protect personal information  —  free AI tools aren’t private by default. Beneficiary and donor data stays out unless the platform is secure and configured for it.
02  Keep a human in the loop  —  until you fully understand how a tool behaves on your work, review outputs before relying on them.
03  Customise for your community  —  out-of-the-box AI doesn’t reflect every context. Adapt it to truly serve the people you serve.
The shortest path from blank prompt to useful output.
Match the model to the task — and spend tokens like the budget they are.
Use it for  hard reasoning, ambiguous problems, gnarly code, the tricky first draft.
Such as  Claude Opus · ChatGPT’s deep-reasoning mode.
Powerful models cost more per message — save them for when they earn it.
Use it for  everyday drafting, summaries, reformatting, repetitive Q&A, quick edits.
Such as  Claude Sonnet or Haiku · ChatGPT’s fast mode.
Most of your day-to-day work never needs the expensive model.
Start a fresh chat for a new task  —  long histories re-send every earlier message, and you pay for them each time.
Be specific, not verbose  —  a clear, short prompt beats a long, rambling one.
Don’t paste the whole document  —  give the one section the model actually needs.
Reuse your saved prompts  —  don’t re-explain the same setup from scratch.
Turn off tools you’re not using  —  web search and extras add tokens you may not need.
Start with the smallest model that could do the job. Escalate only when it visibly struggles — most everyday work never needs the expensive one.
Right-sizing is the cheapest habit you’ll build
Ask AI to solve a real work problem
Draft a grant application for the (fake) grant on the next slide.
The goal isn’t perfection; it’s experimentation.
Can you get AI to speak in your org’s voice? Reflect your mission?
Show off if you know more than prompting already!
There is no single “right” prompt. Type anything. Revise as many times as you want.
You have 10 minutes. Use AI to draft a response to the following:
Describe your organisation and its impact. (2,000 words or less)
How will you use this grant to increase your organisation's impact?
How will you measure the success of your impact?
The goal isn't a perfect application. It's this:
Can you get AI to sound like your organisation? To reflect your mission, your community, your voice?
Did you revise or iterate on your prompt? (If so, how?)
What context did you give the AI about your organization?
If you did more than prompting, what did you do and why?
01  Give it a role and a goal
“You are a [add an expert, such as a "social media expert"] and your objective is to [add goal, such as “create LinkedIn posts about events”]”
To complete these instructions, follow these steps:
“When you are done, write your final response”:
A summary paragraph of the work done
3 bullet points, one per next step
Try your prompt again with this new structure
Start a new conversation and ask it again using these ideas.
You are refining your prompt - not trying to perfect the output.
Role & Goal  —  where did you define the model’s expertise or purpose?
Steps & Context  —  where did you guide how it works with you?
Output  —  where did you specify what “done” looks like?
Which part was hardest to make explicit, and why?
Have it ask you clarifying questions
Have it write the prompt for you
Welcome to the Prompt Cookbook from Decoded Futures!
Stretch, refill, compare notes. Back at the top of the hour.
Privacy & data controls  —  find where to turn off model training on your data. Critical for NGOs handling sensitive beneficiary information.
Attachments, web search, tools  —  see what the input bar can actually do.
Free tools aren’t private by default
Defining AI problems and Solution Types
The friction in your work is the starting point, not the tool.
Fit a challenge into this template
My pain point at work is that I spend time (specific task).
If I could reduce or change this by (specific improvement),
I would gain (time, quality, or clarity impact).
Big challenges and small ones both count — a fundraising goal, or one type of email.
“My pain point is that I spend too much time trying to raise money. If I could fix this by getting more money, I’d gain greater impact.”
A real problem, but not one AI can directly solve. Specificity is missing.
“I spend hours answering repetitive enquiries about our programmes each week. If I automated even half, I’d gain ~5 hours a week for higher-value work.”
Specific task, specific change, measurable gain.
Great! You’ve identified a problem. So how do we know whether AI can actually solve it?
Create & Communicate  —  “I need to write or create something to share with others.”
Learn & Decide  —  “I want to learn something new or need better insight to make smarter choices.”
Answer & Assist  —  “I get a lot of questions and can’t keep up with answering them all.”
Automate the Admin  —  “I keep doing the same task over and over and it’s taking time away from more important work.”
Sort & Scan  —  “I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”
“I need to write or create something to share with others.”
“I want to learn something new, or need better insight to make smarter choices.”
Analysing qualitative survey data about your programmes
Planning whether to expand to a new area
Summarising a long report into a decision
“I get a lot of questions and can’t keep up with answering them all.”
Answering staff questions about a new HR policy
A first-line FAQ for your programmes
Projects are great when you want to:
“I want an AI that answers my and my team’s questions about our company materials.”
Prompt: Describe how it should interact with your team and your materials.
Files: Share the materials you want it to pull from.
THE ORGANIZATION KNOWLEDGE BASE: Build a Claude Project for your organisation
Step 1. Start a new Claude Project and upload a document your team uses regularly.
(No document? Use your organisation's website copy, an old annual report, or your programme brochure.)
Step 2. Write a system prompt telling Claude to only answer questions using that document.
Example: "You are a helpful assistant for [org name]. Answer questions only using the materials provided. If the answer isn't in the document, say so."
Can a new volunteer or donor understand your work just by chatting with this assistant? Could your team use this instead of digging through folders?
How well did the project follow your instructions?
The stronger your data is for the your projects, the better the outcomes will be. Invest time on clean data!
Food, fresh air, and good conversation. You've earned it.
Create & Communicate  —  “I need to write or create something to share with others.”
Learn & Decide  —  “I want to learn something new or need better insight to make smarter choices.”
Answer & Assist  —  “I get a lot of questions and can’t keep up with answering them all.”
Automate the Admin  —  “I keep doing the same task over and over and it’s taking time away from more important work.”
Sort & Scan  —  “I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”
“I keep doing the same tasks over and over, and it’s taking time from more important work.”
Reformatting data from one layout to another
“I have to get all this data from Salesforce into Excel and then transform it.”
“I wish I could visualize / interact with some data in a more human way.”
“I have to ensure all the data in my system is right / complete (e.g. all my grantees filled out every field in the grantee report portal).”
Vanilla Claude Chat can write simple code for you!
Don’t think about it as “coding” - you are merely asking for a computer to do something annoying for you.
The mad lib to start with“Make me a (automation) that can run in my browser” “Make me an HTML file that…”
It’s very helpful to be very specific about the STEPS section of your prompt if your task is specific or complex.
Have Claude build your ideal data interface
Drag a data file you already use into the chat. (Beneficiary records, volunteer attendance, programme reach data, anything you track in a spreadsheet.)
Ask Claude to show you that data the way you actually want to see it:
How do you wish you could see this information?
Which numbers matter most to your team or funders?
How would you want to look at it (graphs, filters, sorting by district or programme)?
Example: "Can you look at our beneficiary data and make an interactive dashboard I can open in my browser, with a map by district and a breakdown by programme type?"
If you don't have data handy:
Use your organisation's publicly available annual report numbers, or ask Claude to generate sample data that looks like yours so you can test the interface first.
Claude Code — when chatting isn’t enough
The same Claude you know, now working directly inside your files, folders and tools.
TWO WAYS TO WORK WITH CLAUDE
It talks  you type, it replies in the chat window.
You move the work  copy text in, copy the answer back out by hand.
Best for  thinking, drafting, summarising, answering questions.
Great for words — you still do the doing.
It acts  reads and edits your real files and runs the task itself.
It moves the work  opens folders, edits many documents at once.
Best for  repetitive, multi-file, “just change it” jobs.
Great for doing — it works where your files live.
How to use it — and where it fits
Point it at a folder  —  open Claude Code where the files you want to work with already live.
Ask in plain English  —  “rename these by date,” “pull the totals into one sheet,” “draft a report from these notes.”
Watch and approve  —  it shows each change before it makes it — nothing happens without your okay.
Use it where you work  —  on your desktop (Mac or Windows), in the editor, in the terminal, or on the web.
Connect your tools  —  link Google Drive, Gmail and more so it works with the data you already have.
Use it in the browser (can’t access your computer)
Use it on your computer with low permissions (Don’t approve anything - just leave)
Use it on your computer with medium permissions (Review every approval)
Use it on your computer with lots of permissions (Approve most things)
“I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”
ROLE AND GOAL: You are an expert technologist hiring manager. Your job is to help me assess the technologists in this spreadsheet.
Score each volunteer on their technology skills
Score each volunteer on how many roles they have around mentorship / advising
Create a visualization of this list of scored candidates that I can run in my browser. Let me sort and filter them by all relevant fields.
Run any problem against the five :-
The right tool follows the problem, never the other way round. Name the work first; the technology second.
GET TO KNOW YOUR TEAM (10 min)
01. What does your organization focus on?
02. What’s a challenge or opportunity you’re hoping AI might help with?
03. What are you thinking about building?
04. What’s your comfort level with AI?
05. What’s one thing that helps you collaborate well with others?
My pain point at work is that I spend time (doing a specific task).
If I could reduce or change this by (specific improvement),
I would gain (time, quality, or clarity impact).
What challenge you set out to work on today
What went well? What was a challenge?
Session 2  —  See you here in one week!
Stay in touch  —  chat on WhatsApp or email the team anytime between sessions.
For next time  —  Think of 1-2 challenges you’re facing that you think AI could help with
Before you go, give us two
Thank you for trusting us with your time and your work. We’re building this together, and this is only Session 1!!
